title: 4 Principles To Use AI Effectively
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/Pih_ICommdY" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Four key points on how to get success out of AI. Point number one, you must understand the task you are trying to accomplish. So, you are delegating. Okay, you need to think about the fact that you're not just chatting with AI, you are delegating work to it. You have to understand how that work is going to happen. You don't have to understand every detail, but you have to understand it structurally. And that way you're going to be able to provide instructions, context, and the way I do all the time is just point it to the necessary context. I know that this is an example of what good looks like, but I want it to do this and this, and here's an example of whatever. You point it to three different examples and explain how you want the new solution to look based on all the examples, what those are for, and it just figures it out, and it works. Point number two, give AI its own space. Okay. So, AI changes things. Okay so yes, the web app has this figured out already. It just works in the web app, it never changes anything. But if you start working with desktop apps, it's going to change things. If you're working with Excel add-in or PowerPoint add-in or whatever you have, it's actually making changes. So, you want to have things backed up, you want to have a copy, you want to give it its own column to work in if it's Excel, you want to give it its own tab, you want to give it its own folder if it's a desktop app, and just make changes within those folders if it's code. Um use GitHub or something similar. It's where it's at. That way you can revert changes that AI makes if it messes up. Um point number three, you can have it search the web for you and learn to understand whatever you're trying to do. But don't get this mixed up with point number one. Those have to happen separate. So, basically split out, hey, I'm learning how this works." versus "Okay, now I'm going to tell you how to do it." So, those are two separate things. A lot of people get this wrong. And point number four, give AI enough energy to complete the task. So, if you have it on instant mode and it's like it tries to complete it in a few seconds and you know, if you're doing a big spreadsheet change, it's going to fail. So, yeah, put it on thinking. Maybe by need thinking extended, maybe need thinking heavy or whatever. Maybe need pro. So, give it enough energy to complete the task and that's my four points. Hope this helps you be more effective when you use AI. Thanks.

title: Was Theranos Right After All

transcript: Shocking news. Elizabeth Holmes was right about the vision. Theronos was right. Yeah, kind of. It's just that she faked it and it was a complete fraud in the end. So yeah, cool vision though. I think she was wrong on the timeline and what I'm saying is AI could actually change this and I think it will. Um now just some ideas on the medical field and what I'm excited about not personally working on the medical field but just the ideas that I'm I find fascinating is so the key with AI is that if you understand something then you can do it basically. I mean AI makes a lot of things easy if you understand how it works and then on the other side of that you can use AI to help you understand things. So, it's kind of a two-part thing. And what people don't usually get is they want AI to just do it because AI can help understand and then you can do what you understand. But the problem is is that that human in the loop is kind of critical because you try to just say, "Hey, go do this." But you don't understand it. Then AI messes things up and nobody understands how it works. So, that's a key issue with a lot of people's AI use. Um, now, as far as the medical field goes, I have these two little thoughts going on. It's just kind of cool. Um, one is cheaper sensors. So I think this is kind of where that whole theos thing was going was that oh you could do some little whatever and it's much cheaper and you could get all this data out of less. Um but my thought on it is actually like what if you took like a Apple Watch or whatever something that took data through your wrist and you took data over time and it could tell so much more. This is just so exciting to me. So I mean it's not really revelation video. It's just I'm just you know talking about this because it's kind of really exciting what might be possible. So if if we added time into the factor of the data, it could probably derive a lot more things about what's going on in your body than you know what the information that it gives. Now, it's just kind of a theory I have. Um, and we might be able to make cheaper sensors or use the existing sensors we have for more things. Um, and then the other side of the the coin is, and I think this is being worked on, I saw a little bit about this is just that um, if you take all the data, you just capture tons of human data. And the part that makes humans so hard is that no human is the same. Everybody is unique. And I think this is actually a feature rather than a bug. I mean obviously humans are fine and it's fine that we're different. But um what I'm saying is you take large language models and the whole concept of a large language model was that you take language and you just predict the next thing. And I think human like just our medical data is similar to that in the sense that it's so vast and everything is so different and all the AI has to do is take all that data and make predictions and I think AI could actually simplify our world model view of how the human body works. Um, like if it could isolate and it could say, "Well, here's a consistent thread that happens through people in a pattern that we never would could have noticed just looking at stuff." Um, that's just what's just so super exciting. So, um, so number one, I hope we can have breakthroughs in the amount of diseases we can cure. And number two, I hope we can make the sensors cheaper that'll figure out what those diseases are, make it more accurate to figure out what's going on with people and fix more health problems. So, that's just super exciting. I'm not working on it, but I see the possibilities. I don't have to be a medical person to be like, "Dude, I could use this

title: Playing The Game of Success
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/GGXUo0bkaU4" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Hello there. Today I'd like to talk about playing the game of success. Now if you are in the working world, you are in some way playing this game. So in order to most effectively play, you need to fluctuate between two um fundamentally different strategies. So the one strategy is going to be spread. And this is kind of where I started thinking about this video was what we can learn from index funds. So basically the tactic of index funds is to spread your risk and eventually you get some kind of reward. Now the problem with that is it works completely against another fundamental strategy of success and that is focus. So what ends up happening is you begin fluctuating between spreading out your risk I guess is what you might say um and then narrowing in to focus on what works. So I guess the main thing you need to know about this strategy is if you have plateaued in your income potential then that means it's time to spread. So you need to think about what is beside your current skill set or beside your current offering. Who else could you um who else could you add value to or whatever it takes to make more money essentially? Um and then if you are if you find yourself neglecting something that's working then it's time to focus. So in other words you have got signal from the market that hey this is a way that I can make money but I'm trying to do 10 other things. Well then it's time to cut off you know three of those or however many it takes. cut off things that are not working so well to focus your time more on what is working. Now, a key thing I want to talk about briefly is signal. So, signal in the marketplace I would define as um pain or sorry, sacrifice by other people, put it that way. Um, so in other words, if somebody's willing to spend money on something that is sacrificed by another person and that means basically proves the value of whatever that channel is that you are working in. So if you have 10 things, I guess the important thing to remember is just that if something feels good, that's not enough to mean that it's something worth focusing on because um other people need to give you the feedback that hey, I'm willing to sacrifice. I'm willing to spend money or spend time or whatever it takes. When other people start start actually sacrificing their time and money for whatever your offering is, then you know that is true value. So you end up fluctuating spread, focus, spread, focus, and you do this your entire life as long as you're playing the game of success. Hope you found this insightful. effects.

title: How Credit Cards Work
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/a4Dm0bgttCg" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Hey guys, I've often wondered how credit cards really worked. So, I decided to make this video to truly understand them. Starting at the beginning, the credit card was born from one of the most relatable moments in human history. Bro forgot his wallet. 1949, a guy named Frank is at a business dinner in New York City. The check comes, no wallet. His wife had to come rescue him. Of course, the natural thing to do. Frank decided to reinvent money. He came back to that same restaurant exactly one year later just to make a point and paid with a small cardboard card. The world's first credit card. That card worked at 27 restaurants in New York City. That's it. That was the product. It was called the Diner Club card. Made of cardboard, not plastic. And you had to pay the full balance at the end of the month. Imagine that. Frank's name was literally on the first one. The idea spread fast. Banks wanted in. In 1958, Bank of America mailed a plastic card unsolicited to over two million people in California. Nobody asked for it. It just showed up in their mailbox. They called it the Bank America card. Bank America card got big, really big. But the name only worked in America. So in 1976 they spun it off into its own independent company rebranded for the whole world. They called it Visa. Same card, new name. Early cards had a major problem. There was no fast way to verify anything. Every transaction was a phone call or paper imprint. Painfully slow. The fix came from an IBM engineer named Forest Perry and more importantly from his wife. Harry was trying to attach a strip of magnetic tape to a plastic card for CIA identity badges. He couldn't get it to stick without warping it. His wife happened to be ironing clothes. She suggested he used the iron. It worked. A household iron. The origin of credit card tech. The stripe itself is basically a tiny strip of cassette tape. Inside it are billions of microscopic iron particles. Each one acts as a miniature compass pointing in one of two directions. It works basically like a barcode. Uh when you swipe, the reader detects the pattern like a needle reading the grooves on a vinyl record and it sends your info to the bank. Fast and simple and also completely flawed. The data never changes. Every swipe broadcast the exact same information. So, criminals figured out they could attach skimmers to ATMs and gas pumps. Capture your stripe, clone your card, use it anywhere. By the 2000s, card fraud was a multi-billion dollar disaster. Something had to change. France saw this coming first. Chip cards debuted there in 1986. Europe was dealing with worse fraud than the US and they moved faster because of it. The problem was every country built their own version of the chip. So they didn't speak the same language. So in 1993, Europe, Visa, and Mastercard joined forces to build one global standard EMV named after the three of them. Americans didn't widely get chip cards until around 2015, one of the last major countries to adopt them. More on the liability shift that changed this in a second. Here's what makes the chip fundamentally different. Instead of broadcasting the same card number every time like the Stripe does, the chip generates a brand new one-time code for every single transaction. a unique encrypted cryptogram specific to that purchase. If a criminal somehow intercepted it, it's worthless. It'll never work again. Like a scratch off lottery ticket, once it's scratched, it's done. So, how does that work? The chip on your card has no battery. It's completely dead when sitting in your wallet. So, how does it compute anything? Well, when you insert your card, the terminal does two things simultaneously. It sends electricity through the metal contacts to power the chip, and it sends a clock signal, basically a heartbeat, that tells the chip how fast to run. Without the clock, the power alone does nothing. That wakes the chip up. Then the chip pulls in a few pieces of information. the transaction amount, a random number, the terminal just generated, and an internal chip count um internal counter that the chip increments every single time it's used. It mixes all that together with a secret key, runs it through an encryption algorithm, and spits out an eight byte code, your one-time cryptogram. Then it goes back to sleep. Now, here's the part that makes the whole thing work. That secret key didn't come from the factory. It was put there by the bank specifically for your card during a process called personalization right before the card was mailed to you. Your bank kept a copy of the same key in its own secret vault. Nobody else has it. Not Visa, not the merchant, no one. So when the cryptogram leaves your card, it travels through the terminal to your to the merchants bank onto the Visa Mastercard network and the network reads the first six digits and that identifies the bank and it routes the cryptogram directly there. Your bank reruns the same math on its end and if the answers match, it's real. And if it they don't, it's fraud. Every other bank in the world is completely blind to this exchange. They don't have your key. They don't need it. Each bank only ever verifies its own cards. The US was one of the last major economies to adopt chips 29 years after France first deployed them. The reason a combination of cost to upgrade infrastructure and for a long time banks just kept absorbing the fraud losses. It was cheaper to eat the fraud than force a national upgrade. The math changed in 2015. So this is where I was confused. I was thinking if the chip is so much more secure, why do cards still have the magnetic stripe? Increasing the attack service makes a card more secure. A chain is only strong as its weakest link, right? Which it's a fair question, but the answer involves a brilliant and ruthless piece of economic engineering called the liability shift. In 2015, when the US pushed chip cards out, millions of businesses still had old swipe only terminals. You can't upgrade every gas station and food truck overnight. So, the card networks made a compromise. Keep the stripe for backwards compatibility, but completely change who pays when things go wrong. Here's the key part people get wrong. The liability shift has nothing to do with tracing where the card got stolen. No investigation. The rule is purely about where the fraudulent char card gets used. Criminal skims your card in Ohio, walks into a shop in Florida with a fake card. That Florida shop is on the hook. Not because they did anything wrong, but they use the weaker technology. So, this confused me. Can a merchant refuse to let you swipe? Because they all have the ability to swipe, of course. Um, sort of. But here's the clever part. The magnetic stripe on a chip card actually contains a hidden flag that tells the terminal, "I have a chip. Use it." So when you try to swipe a chip card, the modern reader detects that code and prompts you to insert instead. A merchant can ignore the prompt and let the swipe go through, but the moment they do and fraud happens, they own it. So, for the long-term plan, the Stripe is just quietly being retired. Starting in 2024, newly issued Mastercard cards are no longer required to have a stripe, and by 2033, no Master Card will have one. Not banned, just uh allowed to die. Just when we figured out the chip, someone asked, "What if you didn't even have to touch the reader?" And the answer came from a bus system in South Korea. The world's first contactless payment launched in 1995 in South Korea. A transit card called UPass for bus commuters. Instead of fumbling with coins or swiping, you just tap and kept moving. The idea was make it so fast and frictionless that people stop even thinking of it as payment. Contactless credit cards came to the US in 2004, but the real tipping point was smartphones. Google Wallet launched in 2011, Apple Pay and Android Pay came in 2014 2015 and that's when Tapay was truly mainstream. Then COVID pushed the adoption even further as people wanted to avoid touching shared services. Contactless payments in the US rose 150% between March 2019 and June 2020. Here's how the tap actually works. The payment terminal broadcasts a tiny invisible electromagnetic field like a bubble of energy just in front of the reader. The moment you bring your card inside that bubble, the field induces a small electrical current in a thin antenna coil hidden inside your card. That current wakes the chip up. No battery, no contact. Think of it like a wireless charger on your phone. The 4 cm range isn't an accident. It's intentional. The signal is so short that you have to be right there, which makes it very hard for someone to silently intercept without you noticing. You have to mean it. So, chip dipping and tapping are the same security technology. The chip generates the same one-time encrypted cryptogram either way. When you tap, the terminal's electromagnetic field powers the chip wirelessly. The same code, same encryption. The only difference is the wire. That's the whole story. Hope you found this informative. Thank you very much for watching. Cheers.

title: Time Stops
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/MGWVEbct7HU" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Hello everyone. Have you ever noticed that time stops with some relationships? In others, time passes quickly. So, if you're apart from whoever it may be and then you get back together, sometimes you don't know the person at all. Basically, it feels like it's been years when maybe it hasn't. I just want to dive into the difference here. So the difference is if things in your life — if something more important than the relationship has come up in your life or happened in your life that's when time happens, as far as your mental clock goes. So basically like if you have let's say your friends as a child with probably a lot of people and so the interesting thing is as we grow older it seems like sometimes we get more isolated because everybody goes their own different path. And so the thing about this is that obviously what we each person perceives as important changes as you grow older. And so you get back together with somebody that you were talking about toy trucks and the latest little video, but then you've had much more important things happened in your life later on. And so your lives have gone in different directions. So, the interesting thing I wanted to go into here is that — talk about those other people where you get back together and you're right back at the same where you left off. And that is when you have a relationship where the level of importance in of the substance of your relationship, how you're whatever you are together about is truly important in the sense of it is as important or more important than anything that happens in between times. So you go about your day-to-day life and then you get back together and you're back at the stage of the depth of your relationship that you were before and mentally time does not pass in those cases. The interesting thing I wanted to get out of this is that there are situations where you're friends with someone at a young age and then you're still very good friends at an older age. And in those cases basically you go through a process of whatever you want to call it but upgrading the relationship or updating the relationship maybe. Basically you bring bring the relationship you start talking about the things that are important in your adult life. And of course you can reminisce about the old days, but I just wanted to talk about this in relation to the trend that you get, as you get older, you may find yourself becoming more isolated because all your classmates, whatever, people go in different directions. But the easiest way to form friendships as you get older, let's say you wanting to overcome that, is to upgrade those relationships that you had before because you already have a relationship. You just have to bring it up to speed with what is important in your life. So this comes to a thing called shared trajectory. So everybody is moving through life in some way and — the so the way to look at it is you may be going in very different directions than people you were friends with at a young age, but you can usually find things that overlap. Maybe — this goes back to another video of the pains and the games where you may feel like, "Oh, my life has gone a completely different direction." But when you go through all the different pains and games in your life and then you think about or you may or may not know what those are for the other person, but you may find an obscure one that you can link on to and bring the relationship up to date or find a way that your lives still are going in the same direction. Maybe some of the things are still important to you both. So, it's just an interesting concept as far as just figuring out why time passes in some relationships and how to make it so that we maintain — where it makes sense, maintain those old relationships to the current level of importance. Anyway, hopefully you found this interesting.

title: Developing personal improvement system (a start)
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/uchJP2QGWbo" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Hey guys, I expect that I'll eventually have a better video about this, but I did want to talk about kind of starting to develop some idea of a personal improvement system. Basically, so I know I did a video about unlocking the why, which talked about pains and gains, and it was really interesting. What I'm finding as I dig through this whole topic is that so the first thing if you define your pains and your games — and then and then what you may find is that you have a pain that is being neglected because your the games what your your strengths what you're really good at. you end up spending too much time on just the things that you find fun and you are neglecting a pain. Be interesting to see — get feedback about what other people find on this. But anyway so the idea there is to if if you have if you discover that you know you are neglecting one of your key pains because you know you're just good at other things, then dig into that specific pain and turn it into a game. So a game is going to be — see what you can do to make the rewards greater. maybe break — and and the — make it easier to play the game. Somehow break the game down into easier easier smaller sizes or whatever make it more playable. So, that's what I've found for myself is just some games some some pains are you think oh it's too hard you know I can't do this. So you just leave it and it continues to be painful. But if you break it down, you can make it playable and you can turn it into a game that you can play and then that really starts to make a difference on your life because you you start to actually address those things you are neglecting. So, yeah, I'm hoping to have a more structured like a maybe do some visual slideshow versions with some cool visuals to kind of show dive into the detail of that. But anyway, it's just so interesting. I wanted to get this out there and see what you all think. Thanks for watching.

title: Data Collection in prep for AI Future
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/qnRDoAbbHDM" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Hey guys, another video here about data capture. So one thing that I as soon as kind of the AI world started to come upon us I started thinking about data capture because you know if you capture all this data and you can't look into all the data yourself but with AI suddenly we can make use of a lot of data and we can get really good insights the more data we have. So, for one example, I'll just talk about these videos. It's one of my motivations to create videos is to capture data. Basically, I start to have kind of a database of all, you know, the different concepts and things I've figured out and a lot of stuff like that. So, I start to have a library of as you might see, I go on my misterautomate.com website and you can see all the transcripts. So, I've automated it to pull the transcripts. And so, basically turning all these videos into text, which text is now an easy way to — you can use text data in a lot of ways. So, another way I've done is put little, activity tracking things and I can start to with with like activity tracker on my computer, you can just pull the raw logs off of that and get some interesting insights into, you know, my my own personal productivity that way. And I guess there's a lot of way a lot of ways you can set up, you know, tracking and logging and different things. I mean, obviously you want everything to be secure. Don't, you know, expose things beyond what you're comfortable with. Because you don't want bad actors to get your data. But I guess just in thinking about being prepared for future possibilities. If you collect data now, you'll have a lot more insight to take advantage of that data that you collected. There are just so many ways to use AI with data. So, it's just an interesting thought for preparing for the future. And hope you found this insightful.

title: Your Business as an API
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/2vKpOrqkyZo" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: One thing I&#39;ve been thinking about a lot lately is APIs for business. So if you think about your business in terms of exposing an API, now API is a basically computers talking to computers. So if you think about your business in that way, it gets really interesting. So basically what questions do your customers have for you and then how do you get those answers and return those answers and how do we prepare our business to be essentially an API for because if we can expose an API that says hey you know I want to place an order whatever and we make it really easy to expose. We literally create an API for our business and it has the answers that a human would do. Well, we can expose that in any way that&#39;s convenient. We can direct integrate with our customers or we can create a customer portal and have the customer portal use that on our website or whatever else. So I think this is we think about the future of business and lots of people using AI agents and if we have an API already, then we&#39;ll be ready for the next phase of business and we&#39;ll make it easier for customers to do business with us. So I think that&#39;s a really interesting thing to start thinking about. You know what? If your business could simply be an MCP server, so your customers hooked up to your system or whatever generated the key they needed to authenticate and then — then they could start sending you requests like order something, check order status, check availability, etc., etc. You just expose a whole bunch of tools to your business of all these questions that people can ask and just get instant answers and you&#39;re literally ready for the agentic world at that point which I think is going to be key for business is preparing for the future.

title: Attention Economy and AI Video
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/NKtfqUsn_Cw" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Okay. So, the attention economy is going to be won by someone using AI video. Now, I don't say exclusively one. Obviously, there's going to be massive competition, but here is where I'm going with that. AI video makes the production side of videos become a lot easier and cheaper. So, we're not going to suddenly lack — like the resources to produce. So, that allows creators to put all their efforts into the storytelling side of the video and engineering emotions. So basically, yeah, somebody's going to figure this out and they're going to win the attention economy without spending a lot of money on production. So yeah, I think there obviously will always be a place for the authentic videos and just it's kind of like sugary foods like people came in and like just designed foods to human appetite. Same thing will happen with video especially since you can kind of do any kind of video now for cheap. So, somebody will figure out how to tune videos to exactly what humans crave. I mean, people have already done this but it's just going to get a lot crazier because the production side is so much cheaper. So yeah, like you see with Sora — the Sora app, people generate all these videos and people watch the videos. Well, it doesn't matter if I mean if you know it's an AI video, it doesn't matter if it's an AI video. It's just that you want to be entertained or you want to feel things or whatever. And so you just have videos that make you feel things. And a good storyteller will do that. And it doesn't matter if it's real video or fake.

title: Why did we go to the moon
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/KGjTfJd20y4" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Why did we go to the moon? Does anybody know why we went to the moon? So, the reason I've been thinking about this is there's a — there's an inspirational I don't even remember where I've heard it, but I've heard it multiple times, little story that people tell, and somebody goes and asks a janitor, I guess, at NASA, you know, what are you doing, whatever, and instead of saying mopping the floor, he says, oh, we're putting a man on the moon. I guess that was probably the Apollo mission times. But anyway, so this whole theme is about you know the people in your organization rather than saying hey here's what I'm doing to know the the bigger purpose and the bigger why behind their organization. Which is inspirational in its own right. So that's great. Not downplaying that at all. But then you get to the question that going to the moon is a what? Not a why. It's just you know a thing you could do. So why why go to the moon? And I thought about this a bit and it is because — in my before I say that in my thinking I'm like okay we do everything in relation to some pain that we are trying to solve or avoid or whatever. So yeah, that's the precursor. It is in order to avoid the pain of stagnation and this is a huge thing in the human race. The human race has ever burning desire to reach the next level in the game. So so that's that's what it's all about. And this this plays right into — you'll see another one of my videos talking about pains and games. So, so that's it. We — we want to play the game. Anyway, yeah, I guess that's all I have for that. It's just interesting thing to think about. Why bother going to the moon? Oh, yeah, I just remembered there are some other reasons to go to the moon. But anyway, you know, all these scientific discoveries, great things. I just wanted to clarify that so you know this doesn't get a bunch of backlash like no that's not the only reason. Of course there's tons of reasons. Yeah, I do. I got it. Cool. Cheers.

title: Unlocking The Why
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/xyNw8JqX_9E" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Hey everyone, I'd like to unlock the why for you today. All right, so let's give you a little bit of background here. What we're looking at is called the golden circle as widely discussed by Simon Sinek. So in case you're not familiar, what this is all about, how he describes it is every organization knows what they do. Some organizations know how they do it and very few organizations know why they do what they do. Now, I'm going to bring it to a personal level and this may impact the conversation a bit. So, I'm just giving you that context up front and let's dive into the why specifically. When you go in search of your own why, what you will find is pains and games. Both sections are very important. Now, let me dive into the game section. Now, so when I say the word games, I am referring to anything that you pursue. So basically anything — you can either be successful at what you do or you can fail. So let me dive into a diagram that'll explain games a bit better for you. So on the bottom axis here, we have the difficulty of the challenge. So if a challenge is simply not difficult enough for where your mind is at, you will find it boring. And obviously you don't spend a lot of time doing boring things. Now if it's too hard, if you get overwhelmed immediately at the thought of trying to overcome this challenge, you just drop it. So in the middle is the sweet spot. So this is the area where it's challenging enough to be interesting and it's not so hard to not be able to succeed. So I'm going to add another axis here and that's going to be the amount of pain relief that comes from the activity at the bottom. We have, so I've just split this middle column up. And so if there's low pain relief, we get things like time waste addictions. An example of this might be like a video game because, they engineer video games so that it starts out easy and then you see success and then it gets a bit harder and a bit harder. So, it's challenging enough to be interesting and it gets right to the level of your own abilities and for that reason you stay engaged. Now, where you find the same kind of engagement in something that truly matters and brings pain relief, you will find your why. Now, sounds easy. Great. Let's dive into my own chart just as an example. Okay, so we're going to fill out my chart here on pains. One thing that pains me a lot is to see somebody doing something manually over and over and over. That's very frustrating and I just get like a emotional response of like guys we got to just automate this. You're doing stupid work kind of thing. And thankfully the AI and automation world is exactly the level of — it fits into my sweet spot. We'll just put it that way. So I it's hard enough to be a challenge, but it I do see success. So it's a lot of fun for me. This kind of thing matches up great. Now, here's where I'm going to push back on the whole Golden Circle thing a bit, and that is that this simply does not encompass my entire identity. I have many more pains and games that I love to play or whatever areas that I love to dive into. So, I'm going to go into another example of another thing that pains me. So, we have politics over truth. And all parties are guilty of this. But for me, I understand why politics has to happen. And so since this is largely outside of my circle of influence, I simply choose not to play. So this is this is kind of a it's going to be one of those on the the far right side. It's too hard. So I guess what I want to say out of this is if you play a game you're not positioned to win, you lose. So basically, choose your games wisely. Now I'm bringing up another one. Surface level society and that is just the frustration of the whole internet culture of likes and all these just little metrics of like oh how many subscribers or how many followers or whatever it is for the platform you're on. And what that breeds is basically very shallow relationships. you might have somebody in the comments section. It's like, "Oh, great. I have, you know, Facebook friend or something." Reason I bring this up, if this video meant something to you, if you found this whole concept interesting, I challenge you to share it with someone and tell them why you found it interesting, what it meant to you, and then have a real conversation with that person. And so I'm going to try to play this game a little bit and maybe have a little bit of influence on this whole surface level society culture. So that's it for today. I hope you found this insightful and I've really enjoyed breaking this down. Have a good one.

title: Gumloop Automated Email
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/tKmJP-OEXGU" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Hello there. I wanted to tell you about a cool little automation I did the other day. Actually it's been a little bit now. And mainly I wanted to try out Gum Loop as a platform for automation. They have some good connectors. So, I'm kind of not going to sing their praises to the treetops, but — but I did do one cool little thing with them, and that was to set up an automated email to come to myself. And so I have it researched on the web and then it sends an email to me and it gives me an update on all of the local events and discounts and different things going on in my local area and it's kind of cool. I've been made aware of some interesting things happening around and I wouldn't have known about it otherwise. So, it actually made a little bit of difference for me now. So, I just want to share that quick little idea that you could possibly try yourself if you wanted to. Cheers.

title: Keep Up With AI
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/-YpRol8m5QQ" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Hey there. I wanted to share an unpopular opinion. That is that you can keep up with AI and it's not even too hard. Okay, let me kind of give you some context behind this thought. So, obviously there is tons of new AI stuff coming out all the time and you can't keep up. That's kind of the trend of the talk. Now, I guess if I'm going to approach it from the idea of like, is there some tool out there that I don't know about that could help me do my job better than I do it? And the answer is of course there is yes. There's so many tools and I haven't tried them all and I etc etc. I don't have the time to try them all. If I tried all the tools, basically I would spend all my time trying tools. That's It's kind of like trying to watch all the YouTube videos because there's way more hours uploaded every day than there are hours in a day. So, it's kind of the reality we live in. But the good thing is is that you can keep up with AI. And the reason I say this, if you have just a few main channels of AI news, then all the significant updates are going to hit the news. So basically, yeah, you see stuff like ChatGPT and I didn't catch on to ChatGPT instantly. I started seeing the word ChatGPT for u probably a month before I clicked on it and was like, what is this ChatGPT thing? What are all these articles thinking about? I mean, what what is this about? Because it just it meant nothing to me when I saw the headlines of it. But eventually, I was like, oh, this crazy new tool and this is going to change everything. And I guess that's where I'm kind of going with this topic is just that if something is significant enough that you know all your competitors are going to take advantage of it and you're going to fall behind. Well, it's probably going to be big in the news. Everybody's going to be talking about it. So, same thing happened again with Open Claw. There's all this stuff about Open Claw and, you know, I looked at it, you know, I was a few days late or whatever. I mean, oh, it was Claudebot. I saw a few things about Claudebot and I was like, I don't know what that is. Eventually clicked on it. It's like, oh wow, this agent that can do things and then I looked I was like, okay, it's it's a security nightmare. So, I didn't do anything with that and there's all kinds of updates of course now coming from the main providers, Claude, Chat, GPT, and of course, Google's going to be trying to catch up as well with agents that do things for you. That's obviously where all this is going. And so as long as you're kind of watching, you see what tools are out there, then you know, you might be a month or two late, but it's not going to be the end of the world. In that sense, you're not going to lose some major competitive edge because you found out about a tool one month later. Like that's not how fast customers change who they're buying from just because another company happened to take on and utilize the new tool. That kind of thing just doesn't matter. So that's my talk for today. All the scare about, you know, you're going to fall behind. There's definitely truth to it. There's definitely truth to it if you ignore it. But if you are somewhat interested and you kind of keep up with the tools and you try new things, I guess that's the most important thing of the current age is to check the big updates and look into what they're about and see if it's something just have some general idea of what's going on and if something big happens, you'll eventually catch on to it. If you are staying the news sources. Cool. That's it for today.

title: Spring Cleaning
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/HoGdiOisQJE" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: How's it going? I was doing some spring cleaning recently and I wanted to share some little tips of things I figured out. So, first of all, obviously, you want to clean out everything's garbage. So one of the things I figured out about this is if you move everything, so let's say you're cleaning out a drawer and you move everything in that drawer to another place, it becomes splashingly obvious, really quickly. You'll see things that are just obviously garbage and you kind of forgot they existed. So I guess the tip there is just to move everything out of the space that you are organizing. Secondly is going to be digitize. That's a commonly known one, but it should be noted and there's a lot of good apps that you can just scan a picture in and make a PDF out of something and upload it to the cloud and so you have all these papers and you can just digitize them. The third one is the most interesting that is consumables that have fallen out of the stream of consumption. And so this is going to be like all those things in your freezer that you forgot about. So it's something you need to eat, but you just don't have like a date when you're actually going to eat them. And so they end up getting thrown away sometimes. up. Another good example is any kind of fancy gift consumable item that you just need to use and maybe you're saving it for a special occasion. So, I just want to say this, today is a special day. So, that's your inspiration to start using up those things that you're saving for some special occasion that doesn't specifically exist. Of course, if you are saving it for an occasion that is actually on the calendar, then that's fine. But if it's just kind of tucked away to some unknown, let's do this someday, we forgot about it, then you should just get those things out and use them. Whether it's like a candle or whatever, you know, things like that. Hope this helps in your spring cleaning journey. Cheers.

title: Brutal Feedback
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/f4H4cPeB-UM" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: A random concept I've just been thinking about is what if everyone could take brutal feedback and just like, hey, that's fine. You know, just bash me over the head. It's just an interesting thought. I I started thinking about this when I watched something where it was like, oh yeah, kids will give brutal feedback. And it's very true. Like kids don't have kind of the social knowledge of like, hey, you're going to say something that's going to hurt somebody. But at some point along the way children start to learn the impact of what feedback they're giving and they start to realize ah maybe I'll hurt this person so I don't want to hurt you know their feelings and so then all of a sudden we filter out all that feedback that we could give people and what that results in is that suddenly we don't get the insight into ourselves. that we probably really need. Of course, there's obviously a reason for how this works because obviously if we just felt bad things about ourselves all the time, we wouldn't perform at a high level. We would just be, you know, get depressed or whatever because, you know, just, oh yeah, I'm a terrible person. But if we — if we could take feedback — I guess I think about the feedback loop a lot because the feedback loop is so critical — I mean especially I think about with automating things and you know you got to have a feedback loop say okay the automation didn't work here and so okay let's tweak it and then it gets better and better and better. But it obviously applies with human just everything human as well. So like getting that feedback is so important about how do we how do we get the level of feedback that we need to become our best version of ourselves possible. Anyway, so yeah, so I started thinking, you know, what if we just could take feedback and, you know, just take it with no with no hard feelings. It's an interesting thought because then we wouldn't have learned. We would just continue giving the feedback, of course. hearing all these fun

title: Video Automation Update
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/r_ecyy5VXj4" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript: Okay, just a quick update on my automation that I've set up for the upload. So, I have done an additional step. So still I put it in a folder and then that automatically gets uploaded and then the additional step I've done is it well it continues to get posted to my website and then I have an API call go out to get the transcript of the video and then post that on the website and now as you can see there's a search bar where you can search the transcripts of every video that I post. So, I'm pretty excited about this update because now I've got all the text of the transcripts of all the videos posting to my website as well. So, just some fun automation stuff. I love playing around with automations. See how little work we can do for how much of value. So, that's today's update.

title: Is plain chat the best?
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/oRjZ1WQjigs" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript:
One thing I found interesting: I've tried a lot of things — custom GPTs, skills, various AI tools — and I found myself going back to just plain chat sometimes. Even just the auto, no-thinking model for a lot of questions. There's not really a right or wrong answer — some tasks definitely need thinking capabilities or more reasoning. But I guess what I take from this is that sometimes I've tried to automate something before it really needed to be. Like, let's make a custom tool just for this — but really, plain chat would work fine.

I guess it goes back to something I keep running into: do it manually first. That's the key rule I've come up with for automation. If you do something manually, you deeply understand how it works, and so you know what it's going to take to automate it.

I feel like I've jumped to automating everything before it really needed to be. A takeaway for myself: see the repetition maybe three or four times before looking to build a special tool — because I keep doing the exact same thing over and over, and at that point it makes sense to automate. But yeah, I'm probably just a bit too jumpy to automate everything. Anyway, it's a lot of fun.

title: AI Agent Capability Self-Awareness
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/SVwk9nLQECc" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript:
In the age of AI, most ideas take me about one afternoon to accomplish. But the idea of taking a video, uploading it to a folder, and having it automatically show up on my website has taken me two afternoons. Big idea, I guess — apparently in modern calculations.

Anyway, we'll see if this shows up as expected.

I want to take a minute to talk about a video I saw just recently — it's been circulating around. A guy took ChatGPT Voice and asked it to start a stopwatch. Then there was a reaction from Sam Altman. He said he watched the video and said, "Oh, that feature is about a year out" — because ChatGPT Voice had said, "Okay, I'm starting a stopwatch." Then he said, "Okay, stop it." And ChatGPT said, "Okay, you took seven minutes" — but it had only been like 10 seconds. ChatGPT was just making it up. I was able to duplicate the same issue.

The interesting thing: Sam Altman was focused on the missing tool — the stopwatch. The ChatGPT Voice model simply does not have a stopwatch. That's different from how I think about the issue, because I'm in the middle of designing agents as well, and I run into this exact same problem. An agent says, "Oh, you want to do this? Sure, I can do that" — and then goes ahead and pretends like it will. In my case, it was gathering a bunch of information, and then you get to the end and it fails, because the agent didn't know what it was capable of doing.

The way I see it: go in there and tell your model it doesn't have a stopwatch capability yet, so it doesn't accept requests for tools it doesn't actually have.

Anyway, I just thought that was interesting. It's very relevant to what I do — we have to make these agents know what they're capable of so they don't annoy users. User experience is key in designing tools. That's my thoughts for today.

title: Hello World
embedHtml: <iframe width="480" height="360" src="//www.youtube.com/embed/TMyqjOFRVEc" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
transcript:
Hello world. What a technical pun.

The purpose of this video has nothing to do with being fancy or getting views — it has everything to do with proving that an automated workflow of uploading a video to YouTube will work.

We'll see if this shows up on my channel. If it does, then we know the first hello world experiment was successful. That's what this is all about.

My theory is that it should be very easy to upload new videos — I'm going to try to automate the whole process, so it's just a drag into a folder.

Alright, that's it for my hello world video.
